# The Case for Boring AI

<!-- 
Main argument: Generative AI hype has limited library imagination about AI possibilities
Key message: Most valuable AI for libraries is "boring" task-specific models
-->

## How Generative AI Hype Limits Library Imagination

- ChatGPT's arrival in late 2022 shaped library AI discourse
- Natural response: "How can we chat with our collections?"
- This fixation overlooks vast landscape of practical applications
<!-- Consider adding: Timeline of library AI projects pre/post ChatGPT -->
<!-- Example institutions that pivoted to chatbots -->
<!-- Cost comparisons: chatbot vs task-specific implementations -->

## The Ferrari Problem

- Quote from ModernBERT team about Ferrari vs Honda Civic [@modernbert2024]
  - Link: https://huggingface.co/blog/modernbert
  - Ferrari = GPT-4 (impressive but high maintenance)
  - Honda Civic = specialized models (practical and everywhere)
- Libraries trying to maintain Ferraris when they need Civics
<!-- Could expand: Specific maintenance costs (compute, expertise, updates) -->
<!-- Real examples of failed "Ferrari" projects in GLAM -->

## The Chatbot Trap

![The default "quick fix" approach to AI in libraries](../assets/workshop-slides/quickfix.jpg){fig-alt="Meme showing a quick fix solution" width=60%}

### Default library AI projects:
- Chatbot for catalog
- AI research assistant  
- Conversational discovery tool

### Why these fail:
- **High complexity, low impact**
  - Complex NLU requirements
  - Dialogue management challenges
  - Edge case handling
  - Computational resources
  - Serves relatively few users
  
- **Solving the wrong problem**
  - Users don't need to chat with catalogs
  - Real problems: incomplete metadata, no OCR, poor classifications
  <!-- Add: User research data on actual pain points -->
  <!-- Statistics on chatbot usage vs traditional search -->

## The Power of Boring

### Examples of "boring" but transformative tasks:

- **Document Type Classification**
  - Letter vs report vs memo vs form
  - Enables faceted search
  - Current: manual cataloger review
  - Impact: 95% accuracy, thousands/hour
  
- **Language Detection**  
  - Routes to specialists
  - Current: generalist staff scan
  - Impact: 99% accuracy
  
- **Quality Assessment**
  - Scan quality control
  - Current: spot checks
  - Impact: consistent standards
  
- **Sensitivity Screening**
  - Personal information detection
  - Current: page-by-page review
  - Impact: automated flagging

<!-- Add more examples from actual library implementations -->
<!-- Include metrics: time saved, items processed, accuracy rates -->

## Task-Specific Models: The Unsung Heroes

### OCR Models
- Historical typefaces
- Handwriting by era
- Layout preservation
- Tables and marginalia
<!-- Specific models: TrOCR, Tesseract variants, commercial options -->

### Classification Models  
- Domain taxonomies
- Institutional preferences
- Modest hardware requirements
- Millions of items
<!-- Examples: document type, genre, subject area -->

### Extraction Models
- Named entities (dates, people, places)
- Bibliographic references
- Structured data from forms
- Catalog card layouts
<!-- Tools: spaCy, BERT variants, rule-based systems -->

## Sustainable AI for Libraries

### Advantages of "boring AI":

- **Lower barriers**
  - Standard hardware
  - Established techniques
  - Less specialized expertise
  - Existing workflow integration
  
- **Predictable outcomes**
  - Clear metrics
  - Consistent performance
  - Easy validation
  - Simple maintenance
  
- **Scalable impact**
  - Entire collections
  - Multiple departments
  - Reusable solutions
  - Institutional knowledge

<!-- Cost comparisons with specific numbers -->
<!-- Case studies of successful implementations -->

## Reframing Success

### Measure success by:
- Staff hours reclaimed
- Materials made discoverable
- Metadata consistency
- Processing speed
- Backlog reduction

<!-- Not by: press coverage, technological impressiveness, novelty -->
<!-- Include specific KPIs and measurement strategies -->

## The Path Forward

1. Identify repetitive tasks
2. Look for clear decisions
3. Accept "good enough" (80% rule)
4. Start small
5. Build expertise
6. Share solutions

<!-- Expand each with specific techniques, worksheets, examples -->

## A Different Vision

### Imagine libraries where:
- Automatic quality assessment
- Pre-classification before review
- Overnight transcription
- Content-based suggestions
- Automatic linking
- Real-time screening

<!-- None require chat interfaces -->
<!-- All transform operations -->
<!-- Concrete examples from institutions doing this -->

## Next Steps

<!-- Transition to framework chapter -->
<!-- How to identify these opportunities -->
<!-- Workshop methodology preview -->

---

<!-- 
Additional topics to consider:
- Environmental impact (small models vs large)
- Equity issues (who can afford Ferraris?)
- Skills development for library staff
- Open source vs commercial solutions
- Collaboration opportunities between institutions
- Funding models for boring vs exciting AI
-->

<!-- 
References to add:
- ModernBERT blog post
- Library case studies
- Task-specific model papers
- ROI analyses
-->
